projects.name: MENA to the Valley 2026
projects.company_brief: Sprintly Partners' flagship founder bridge program is now managed as a live operating tracker across cohort selection, funding follow-up, founder onboarding, mentor programming, invitee management, marketing, program management, and partner coordination. This update preserves the earlier founder-pipeline improvements while restructuring the project around the visible workbook tabs from the shared Excel file. It is a workbook-structure update derived from visible tab metadata, not a row-perfect sync of underlying spreadsheet cells.
projects.key_metrics: 23 applications, 12 deposit requests sent, 10 visible workbook workstreams mapped into VSDR structure

timeline.milestone: Early planning checkpoint captured as its own workbook-era milestone bucket
Applications collected and startup scouting pipeline assembled across 23 submissions
12 pre-selected founders received deposit confirmation requests with $4,000 deadline mechanics
Program structure expanded in VSDR to reflect scouting, funding, partnerships, VC, mentors, marketing, invitees, and program management
Virtual program, mentor scheduling, and partner coordination run in parallel
Silicon Valley immersion timing remains dependent on founder confirmations and logistics readiness

next_actions: Reconnect the live workbook to replace structure-level assumptions with row-level statuses where needed
Populate partner, scouting partner, mentor, invitee, and funding rows from the authenticated workbook once access is restored
Reconcile each of the 12 deposit-requested founders into paid, pending, declined, or no-response status using the live tracker
Assign mentor tracks and virtual-program sessions for confirmed founders across AI, GTM, fundraising, and market access
Lock partner-facing marketing beats, invitee outreach, and immersion logistics against the program calendar
possible_intros: Partner bucket: CBE Fintech Hub, SVB, QDB, and similar institutional bridge partners should sit in the Partnerships / VC structure
Scouting partner bucket: ecosystem operators that can source founders across MENA should be tracked separately from corporates and VCs
Mentor bucket: TechWadi mentors and Silicon Valley operators should be tracked as a dedicated mentor pool, not mixed into general intros
Invitee bucket: event or showcase invitees should be managed as their own outreach stream tied to marketing and program milestones
Marketing bucket: showcase amplification partners such as Startup Grind-style community channels should map to program promotion, not startup sourcing
projects.notes: This project was restructured again using only workbook-level information visible from the shared Excel link, specifically the tab names Startup Scouting, Funding, Partnerships (Corporates), VC Partners, Yousra, Invitees, 8th Jan, Mentors, Marketing, and Program Management. I could not inspect the underlying workbook cells from this environment, so this is intentionally not a row-perfect spreadsheet sync. Instead, I preserved the existing founder-pipeline and deposit-tracker improvements, then expanded the VSDR project into a more useful planning structure that mirrors the visible workbook categories. Partner, scouting partner, mentor, invitee, funding, marketing, and program-management sections are therefore structural buckets inferred from tab metadata and should be treated as planning scaffolding until the authenticated workbook can be reviewed directly.

projects.name: ARPU Plus / CloudCom
projects.company_brief: ARPU Plus is a telecoms value-added services company under A15. Sprintly is supporting US business development and market expansion for portfolio products, with current focus on CloudCom / CloudCom Verify. CloudCom appears to be a broader omnichannel CPaaS and mobile identity platform, with Verify as the authentication wedge product.
projects.key_metrics: US positioning + target account mapping underway

timeline.milestone: Agreement signed
Initial CloudCom / Verify research brief completed
Deep-dive diligence pass completed on CloudCom category, market, and competitive set
Positioning questions sent to Shady Aziz and ARPU Plus team
Shady replied inline with customer reference and positioning answers
Target accounts and introductions for US business development

next_actions: Build priority target account list based on OTP cost, identity pain, and hard-market coverage
Map Yousra warm intros to best-fit fintech, telecom, and mobile-first enterprise prospects
Extract hard metrics and case studies from CloudCom deck / slides
Decide whether Verify is sold as standalone wedge or broader CloudCom platform story
Clarify the first US vertical and exact use case to lead with
Prepare a one-page external positioning summary for outreach
possible_intros: Already in conversation: Synapse Analytics — active relationship / business conversation already in motion
Already in conversation: Money Fellows — active relationship / business conversation already in motion
Already in conversation: e& — active relationship / business conversation already in motion
Already in conversation: Contact — active relationship / business conversation already in motion
Already in conversation: Silicon Valley Bank — active relationship / business conversation already in motion

Reached out: Mar Perez (Commercial Bank of California) — innovation economy / banking relevance — LinkedIn outreach sent, awaiting reply
Reached out: Enji Hamed (Amazon) — vendor / marketplace lens — LinkedIn outreach sent, awaiting reply
Next: Tosh Ernest (Silicon Valley Bank) — banking emerging innovation segments relevance
Next: Sara Rona (Silicon Valley Bank) — senior banking decision-maker relevance
Next: Alex Kearns (Citizens Private Bank) — innovation banking relevance
Next: Yousra Abdelghany (Amazon Payment Services) — payments / GCC relevance
Next: Dalia Seif (TikTok) — strategic partnerships relevance
Next: Rania Hadi (Huawei) — developer and startup programs relevance
projects.notes:
Consolidated account view: CloudCom appears to be more than a single auth tool. Best current read is an omnichannel CPaaS + mobile identity platform, with Verify as the likely wedge product for US conversations. What they seem to sell in plain English is lower OTP cost, less login and onboarding friction, stronger authentication, and better coverage in hard-to-reach markets. The category is real and maps to Telesign, Prove, TMT ID, Vonage, Twilio, Infobip, and Open Gateway-style number verification.

What Shady Aziz confirmed by email: we can mention Google and local banks externally; Meta, Xiaomi, FedEx, and Amazon can only be described as indirect partners. For proof, he pointed us back to the product slides for impact / pain-point evidence. On competitors, he said they sometimes collaborate with aggregators by providing coverage, and sometimes compete directly for the same client. Their strongest differentiator is coverage, especially in difficult destinations across MENA, Asia, and Africa. On the commercial pain point, his clearest answer was that OTP can cost up to $0.30 in some markets, so cost + coverage is a sharper story than generic security language.

Current best positioning: CloudCom helps enterprises reduce OTP cost and improve authentication coverage, especially in high-cost and harder-to-reach markets. That is more concrete than describing them vaguely as an identity platform. The strongest story is not just fraud reduction. It is coverage in hard markets, lower OTP spend, and smoother mobile verification.

Intro / connection history is now tracked in three buckets. Already in conversation: Synapse Analytics, Money Fellows, e&, Contact, and Silicon Valley Bank. Reached out: Mathilda Akintayo (Uber), Hamed Rabah (Microsoft for Startups), Ahmed Nouh (Amazon), Waleed Abdelsalam (Huawei Northern Africa Region), Joyce Zhang (Visa), Mar Perez (Commercial Bank of California), and Enji Hamed (Amazon). Next wave: Tosh Ernest (Silicon Valley Bank), Sara Rona (Silicon Valley Bank), Alex Kearns (Citizens Private Bank), Yousra Abdelghany (Amazon Payment Services), Dalia Seif (TikTok), and Rania Hadi (Huawei). This needs to stay visible in the project so we know what already moved versus what is still pending.

What still needs answering: which US vertical to target first, which exact use case to lead with, whether Verify is sold standalone or as part of the broader CloudCom platform, stronger hard metrics from case studies, clearer pricing logic, implementation effort, and what we should avoid overpromising.

Commercial context: Retainer model with revenue share on partnerships introduced. Sprintly role is US business development and market expansion support for ARPU Plus portfolio products, starting with CloudCom.

projects.name: Money Fellows US Market Entry
projects.company_brief: Money Fellows is exploring US market entry with Sprintly supporting market strategy, segment prioritization, regulatory diligence, and go-to-market planning for a focused launch path.

timeline.milestone: US Market Entry strategy meeting completed, target segments and workstreams confirmed
Licensing and regulatory research for US entry

next_actions: Confirm the named Money Fellows-side single point of contact for day-to-day coordination; until then, keep Yousra as the active Sprintly coordination owner based on the Apr 20 meeting context
Prioritize Muslim Americans and immigrant Rosca communities as the first US beachhead segments, with Sharia-compliant positioning at the center of the narrative
Translate the four validated segments into a ranked recommendation set: Muslim Americans, immigrant Rosca communities, credit invisible Americans, and gig workers
Build the financial model covering launch budget, ROI timeline, customer economics, and pricing strategy
Run US licensing and regulatory research to define the market entry path, constraints, and sequencing
Develop a localized marketing plan using native language, community terminology, and community-event-based acquisition tactics
Draft the final strategy document for next week with segment recommendations, financial model, and marketing plan
projects.notes:
Operating truth from the Apr 20, 2026 recap: the viable US target segments are immigrant Rosca communities, credit invisible Americans, gig workers, and Muslim Americans. The strongest initial focus should be Muslim Americans and immigrant Rosca communities, with a clear Sharia-compliant emphasis and messaging that feels culturally native rather than translated. Go-to-market should be localized through the actual terms these communities use and through community events and trusted channels, not generic mass-market performance marketing. The strategy now requires two parallel workstreams before external execution: a financial model covering budget, ROI timeline, and pricing logic, and a licensing/regulatory workstream to determine how Money Fellows can legally and practically enter the US market. The output due next week is a clean strategy document that recommends segment priority, includes the financial model, and lays out the marketing plan.

Team structure update: the client-side working team is captured as the Money Fellows team, while Berkeley is recorded as Sprintly's supporting team on this workstream. Based on the verified meeting/calendar context currently available in the workspace, Yousra is the clearest confirmed active coordination owner because she hosted the Apr 20 meeting and is the only explicitly verified participant in the source recap. The named Money Fellows-side day-to-day point of contact still needs explicit confirmation, so the project record keeps that wording careful rather than overstating certainty.
